methods.description: Process for analyzing bone surface modifications (BSM) on hominin postcranial bones using various tools and techniques to create 3D models and perform quantitative analysis.

steps.description: Pobiner used a 10X hand lens and bright, high-incident light to look for bone surface modifications on hominin postcranial bones at the National Museums of Kenya.
steps.searchable_summary: inspect bones for surface modifications using a hand lens and light

steps.description: She used Coltene President Plus light body dental molding material to create an impression of the marks analyzed in this study.
steps.searchable_summary: create dental mold impressions of bone marks

steps.description: 3-D models of each of the 11 studied BSM were created from an impression of the surface taken from the area of interest on the tibia using a Nanovea ST400 white-light non-contact confocal profilometer.
steps.searchable_summary: create 3D models from bone impressions using a profilometer

steps.description: Processing and analysis of the 3D model was carried out using Digital Surf's Mountains software following Pante et al. Processing included removing outliers, filling in missing data points, and removing the underlying form of the bone.
steps.searchable_summary: process 3D models to remove outliers and fill missing data

steps.description: Data collected through the analysis from the entire 3-D model of the BSM were volume, surface area, maximum depth, mean depth, maximum length, and maximum width.
steps.searchable_summary: analyze 3D models to extract volume, surface area, and depth metrics

steps.description: Additional data were collected from a profile taken from the deepest point of the BSM including area of the hole, depth of the profile, roughness (Ra), opening angle, and radius of the hole.
steps.searchable_summary: collect additional profile data from deepest point of BSM

methods.description: Statistical procedures used to compare experimental BSM data with known samples using transformations and discriminant analysis.

steps.description: These data were statistically compared with a sample of 898 BSM of known origin, including cut marks, tooth marks, trample marks, and percussion marks.
steps.searchable_summary: compare experimental BSM data with known samples

steps.description: Surface area and depth of the profile were excluded from the statistical analyses because they are highly correlated with volume and maximum depth, respectively.
All experimental data were transformed using the Box-Cox method to normalize the distributions for each variable.
steps.searchable_summary: apply Box-Cox transformation to normalize data

steps.description: Comparisons were carried out using the quadratic discriminant function in JMP® statistical software.
steps.searchable_summary: perform quadratic discriminant analysis using JMP

steps.description: The accuracy of the quadratic function in correctly classifying the experimental BSM was 84% when using a 25% validation set.
steps.searchable_summary: validate discriminant model accuracy with validation set

steps.description: Prior probabilities were set equal to the occurrence of each mark type in the dataset to offset the disproportionate representations of each mark type in the experimental sample.
steps.searchable_summary: adjust prior probabilities to balance mark type representation
